SNAP.title: Australian social media ban marred by weak platform checks, tech providers say

macro_events.event_summary: Trump refuses to lift a US-enforced Strait of Hormuz blockade, threatening ~20% of global seaborne oil flows and sending European equities lower.
primary_drivers: Crude oil supply shock / spike in Brent and WTI prices
Shipping and tanker rate surge due to rerouting and war-risk premiums
Risk-off flight to defense, energy, and safe havens; pressure on oil-consuming sectors (airlines, chemicals)

beneficiaries.rationale: Integrated major with large upstream exposure; higher Brent directly flows to E&P earnings. Minimal Gulf production at risk relative to price upside on its global barrels.
Similar upstream leverage to crude; Permian/US-weighted production benefits from price spike without Hormuz transit risk.
Pure-play Permian E&P with no Middle East exposure — maximum torque to WTI price spike from Hormuz disruption.
Scorpio Tankers — product tanker rates surge on Hormuz rerouting around Cape of Good Hope; tonne-miles expand sharply.
Frontline VLCC/Suezmax fleet directly benefits from war-risk premiums and rerouting; crude tanker spot rates historically spike 3-5x on Hormuz scares.
Lockheed — missile defense (THAAD, PAC-3) and F-35 demand accelerates on active Middle East conflict; direct supplier to Gulf allies ramping procurement.
Cheniere — US LNG exports become premium-priced substitute for Qatari LNG that transits Hormuz; European buyers pay up for Atlantic Basin cargoes.
Cenovus — Canadian heavy oil producer with zero Gulf exposure; TMX pipeline gives Pacific export optionality as Asian buyers scramble for non-Hormuz barrels.

losers.rationale: Delta — jet fuel is ~25% of operating costs; unhedged exposure to crude spike compresses margins immediately. Also transatlantic demand hit from European risk-off.
United — heaviest international long-haul exposure among US carriers, including Gulf-adjacent routes that may require rerouting; fuel cost shock compounds.
Southwest — fuel hedges have been reduced in recent years; 100% domestic means no international offset, pure cost hit.
Dow Chemical — ethylene/polyethylene margins compress on naphtha and natural gas feedstock inflation tied to crude; European ops directly hit.
Carnival — bunker fuel cost spike plus likely Mediterranean/Middle East itinerary cancellations; consumer discretionary under risk-off.
FedEx — jet fuel surcharge lag hurts near-term margins; global air freight network exposed to Gulf rerouting and fuel spike.
Honda ADR — Japanese autos sell into Europe and rely on Gulf-transit crude for domestic refining; demand destruction risk from European recession impulse.
AB InBev — European-listed consumer staple with significant EU revenue exposure facing broad European equity selloff and consumer confidence hit.

headlines: European stocks set to open lower as Trump refuses to lift Strait of Hormuz blockade
